notes: This file contains two independent plans: (A) Docker/SQLite crash fix (TASK-1 through TASK-5), and (B) Growth Area Explorer methodology & data pipeline (GA-1 through GA-6). They are independent and can be worked in any order.
'agent' values use opencode.json's roles (planner/coder/test-writer) per your existing config.

Workflow A (Discovery) has been completed: discovery-brief.md, specification.md (updated §1.1), design.md (updated Appendix A) are ready. This task list documents the implementation path but should not be built until the user confirms the methodology direction.

confirmed_root_cause.claim: CONFIRMED via docker compose logs: sqlite3.OperationalError: unable to open database file. BASE_DIR (investor_app/settings.py line 9) resolves to /app. Dockerfile COPY . . runs as root, then only 'chown -R app:app /app/.runtime' is run before USER app — /app itself stays root-owned, so the non-root 'app' user cannot create /app/db.sqlite3.
confirmed_root_cause.confidence: High — confirmed by actual exception text, not inferred.
confirmed_root_cause.fix: Change 'RUN mkdir -p /app/.runtime/matplotlib && chown -R app:app /app/.runtime' to 'RUN mkdir -p /app/.runtime/matplotlib && chown -R app:app /app' in the Dockerfile.
confirmed_root_cause.trade_off_note: This grants the app user write access to the whole /app tree, not just the DB file. Acceptable for an alpha; if that's a concern later, use a dedicated chowned data/ subdirectory and point DATABASES at it instead — bigger change, not needed right now.

tasks.title: [DONE] Capture and report the full, unmuted crash traceback
acceptance_criteria: CONFIRMED: sqlite3.OperationalError: unable to open database file, raised via Django's ensure_connection -> get_new_connection -> Database.connect chain
Container status showed 'Running (0 seconds ago)', consistent with a crash-loop under restart: unless-stopped

tasks.title: Fix Dockerfile ownership so the app user can write the SQLite DB
acceptance_criteria: Change `RUN mkdir -p /app/.runtime/matplotlib && chown -R app:app /app/.runtime` to `RUN mkdir -p /app/.runtime/matplotlib && chown -R app:app /app` in the Dockerfile
Rebuild image (`docker compose build`) and re-run `docker compose up` — confirm gunicorn starts, container status stays 'Up' (not restarting), and http://localhost:8000/ responds

Note the accepted trade-off: this grants the app user write access to the whole /app tree, not just db.sqlite3 — acceptable for alpha; revisit with a dedicated data/ dir + updated BASE_DIR-relative DATABASES path later if tightening permissions matters

tasks.title: Add a real container-startup test that would catch this class of bug
acceptance_criteria: tests/test_container_startup.py currently only asserts string content of the Dockerfile/entrypoint/render.yaml — it does not actually build or run the container

Add a test (marked slow/integration, skippable in fast CI runs if needed) that actually builds the image and runs `docker run --rm <image> python manage.py migrate --noinput` (or equivalent) as the non-root user, asserting exit code 0 — this would have caught the actual failure, unlike the existing string-matching tests

If a full docker build in CI is too slow/costly for your setup, at minimum add a test that checks file-system write permissions for the 'app' user against wherever the SQLite file will be created — flag this trade-off explicitly rather than silently skipping real verification

tasks.title: Fix healthcheck path mismatch between Docker and Render configs
acceptance_criteria: Dockerfile/docker-compose.yml healthcheck hits /api/health/ ; render.yaml's healthCheckPath is /health/ ; core/urls.py only registers a health_check view at /health/ (not /api/health/) — make Docker/Compose consistent with the actual URLconf
This is a separate, lower-priority bug — does not block TASK-1/2, but silently marks the container 'unhealthy' even if gunicorn is running

tasks.title: [DISCOVERY] Explore investment-area methodology and document findings
acceptance_criteria: discovery-brief.md exists with persona, JTBD, riskiest assumption, acceptance criterion, DORA mapping, and prior art analysis
specification.md §1.1 documents the Growth Area Explorer methodology with factor weights, data sources, granularity, and known gaps
design.md Appendix A documents the purchase pipeline architecture, data source decisions, scoring methodology, and risk items
Key decisions (GrowthArea vs MarketSnapshot, crime data source) are resolved or deferred with rationale
Workflow STOPS at plan — no implementation code is written

tasks.title: Diagnose why GrowthArea and MarketSnapshot are empty (0 rows in DB)
acceptance_criteria: Run `python manage.py populate_growth_areas --state=CA --city="San Francisco"` and capture full stdout/stderr — report whether Census/BLS API calls succeed or fail
If API calls fail: identify whether it's an auth error (key permission), rate limit, API endpoint change, or data format change
If API calls succeed: confirm GrowthArea rows are created and data flows through
Alternatively: test the POST flow at /growth-explorer/ via curl or browser with debug logging enabled

tasks.title: Fix None-safety in composite_score and view logic
acceptance_criteria: `GrowthArea.composite_score` property handles None values gracefully (returns None or skips the factor)
`growth_explorer` view validates that population_growth_rate, employment_growth_rate, median_income_growth are not None before computing composite_score or upserting into DB
Test added for None-in-factors scenario
Existing tests still pass

tasks.title: Improve empty-state and error UX for growth area pages
acceptance_criteria: growth_explorer.html shows actionable guidance when GrowthArea and MarketSnapshot are both empty (e.g., 'Select a state and click Analyze State to discover growth areas via Census/BLS data')
growth_areas.html shows a link to /growth-explorer/ when empty, rather than just 'No market snapshots available'
API failure mid-flight shows a per-place or summary error message, not silent omission
Form submit button re-enables on network error/timeout (JS error handling)

tasks.title: Run populate_growth_areas to pre-seed the DB or verify the POST flow end-to-end
acceptance_criteria: After running `python manage.py populate_growth_areas` (with all 73 cities, or a representative subset), GrowthArea table has >0 rows
/growth/ page shows data (not 'No market snapshots available')
/api/v1/real-estate/growth-areas?state=CA returns meaningful results
OR the POST flow at /growth-explorer/ works end-to-end — user selects state, submits, sees ranked results

tasks.title: Update ACS_VINTAGE to auto-detect latest available vintage
acceptance_criteria: ACS_VINTAGE and ACS_VINTAGE_PRIOR are no longer hardcoded as '2022'/'2017'
The integration queries Census API to discover the latest available ACS5 vintage at runtime
Vintages are cached (e.g., for 24 hours) to avoid redundant API calls on every request
Existing tests still pass (may need to update mock expectations)

tasks.title: Add supply-constraint proxy (building permits) to GrowthArea scoring
acceptance_criteria: Fetch building permit data from Census BPS (Building Permits Survey) for each place — total units permitted per year
Compute a supply-constraint ratio: (new units permitted / population growth) — lower ratio = tighter supply = stronger demand signal
Add `building_permits_annual` and `supply_constraint_index` fields to GrowthArea model (use a new migration)
Include the supply constraint in composite_score (weight ~0.10, redistributing from existing factors)
Tests pass for the new data source

summary.blocking_task: GA-1 (diagnose empty DB) is the highest-priority and blocks GA-2/GA-3/GA-4/GA-5/GA-6 — without knowing why data is empty, any fix is speculative
unconfirmed_items: Whether the Census and BLS API keys in .env actually work (they may be expired, revoked, or lack the right permissions)
Whether the Census ACS 2022 vintage API still returns data in the expected format
Whether the BLS employment growth API returns the expected LAUS series data
Whether the user wants pre-seeded data (management command) or on-demand (POST flow) or both
Whether the revised scoring methodology (with supply constraint) is worth building before confirming the existing factors work
